title: The walk that ends an argument
summary: Explore why walking — alone or with someone you have argued with — seems to help resolve conflict, and what it is about movement and physical space that changes how we think and feel.
topic: The walk that ends an argument
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) exploring the experience of going for a walk after an argument — alone or with the other person. Describe what changes during the walk and why movement seems to help. Use present perfect, conditionals, modal verbs, and at least one sentence with 'although' or 'even though'.
hintStarters: Even though nothing had been resolved when I left the room, by the time I turned the corner…
The argument had been building for days, and when it finally broke open I…
What walking does to an argument is not solve it, but…

hintVocab.meaningVi: suy nghĩ lặp đi lặp lại, nhai đi nhai lại trong đầu
có được cái nhìn khách quan hơn
giảm leo thang, hạ nhiệt xung đột
trở nên mềm mỏng hơn, dịu đi
không cần giao tiếp bằng mắt

sampleText:
There is an argument I remember from the third year of my undergraduate degree in Montréal. My flatmate and I had disagreed, badly, about something I can no longer remember clearly — the details have faded in the way that argument details tend to do. What I do remember is that after twenty minutes of going in circles in the kitchen, I said I needed air and left. I walked along the canal for forty minutes, not thinking about anything useful, mostly watching the water and the other people walking their dogs. By the time I got back, I was no longer angry, and I could not have told you exactly when or why.

Although it might seem that walking simply buys time — that the same resolution would have arrived if I had sat quietly in another room — I think something more specific happens. Walking side by side, or even alone, changes the geometry of the conflict. A face-to-face argument keeps you physically oriented toward the other person, which keeps attention on them rather than on the problem. Movement breaks that fixed orientation. Your eyes are looking forward rather than at the person you disagree with, and the rhythm of your steps does something to the internal rhythm of your thoughts. Research from various countries, including studies conducted in Japan and Sweden, has found that people walking together reach agreements more easily than people seated opposite each other. The body's forward motion seems to encourage a kind of forward thinking in the mind.

What I have noticed over many years is that the walk works best when you do not try to use it. If you go out with the deliberate intention of solving the argument, you carry the argument with you and it follows you down the street. The walk that actually helps is the one where you let it go for a while — where you allow yourself to notice the light on buildings, hear pigeons, step around a puddle. When you return, the problem has not disappeared, but you have changed enough that you can see it from a slightly different angle. Even though the conflict may be exactly the same size, you have somehow become a little larger than it, which is all you ever really needed.

cứ lặp đi lặp lại, không tiến triển
không suy nghĩ gì hữu ích

sampleAnnotations.phrase: might seem that walking simply buys time
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: có vẻ như đi bộ chỉ đơn giản là câu giờ

sampleAnnotations.phrase: keeps attention on them rather than on the problem
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: giữ sự chú ý vào họ chứ không phải vào vấn đề
đạt được thỏa thuận dễ dàng hơn

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the deliberate intention of solving the argument
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: ý định có chủ đích để giải quyết tranh cãi

sampleAnnotations.phrase: you carry the argument with you
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: bạn mang theo cuộc tranh cãi bên mình

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Conditional with 'if' + past perfect for a past unreal situation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'the same resolution would have arrived if I had sat quietly in another room' — đây là câu điều kiện loại 3: 'if + past perfect, would + have + past participle'. Nó mô tả một tình huống không có thật trong quá khứ và hậu quả giả định của nó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'the same resolution would have arrived if I had sat quietly in another room' — this is a third conditional: 'would have + past participle, if + past perfect'. It describes an unreal past situation and its hypothetical result. Note the inverted clause order here (result first, then condition).

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'over' + time expression
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What I have noticed over many years' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để mô tả quan sát tích lũy qua nhiều năm đến hiện tại. 'Over many years' là cụm thời gian nhấn mạnh sự tích lũy và tính liên tục, không phải một thời điểm cụ thể.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What I have noticed over many years' uses the present perfect to describe cumulative observation spanning many years up to the present. 'Over many years' is a time expression emphasising accumulation and continuity rather than a specific moment.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một cuộc tranh cãi tôi nhớ từ năm thứ ba đại học ở Montréal. Tôi và người ở chung phòng đã bất đồng gay gắt về điều gì đó mà giờ tôi không còn nhớ rõ — chi tiết đã phai mờ theo cách mà chi tiết tranh cãi thường có xu hướng như vậy. Điều tôi nhớ là sau hai mươi phút cứ xoay vòng trong bếp, tôi nói tôi cần không khí và ra đi. Tôi đi bộ dọc kênh đào bốn mươi phút, không suy nghĩ gì hữu ích, chủ yếu nhìn mặt nước và những người khác đang dắt chó đi dạo. Lúc tôi về, tôi không còn tức giận nữa, và tôi không thể nói chính xác lúc nào hay tại sao.

Mặc dù có vẻ như đi bộ chỉ đơn giản là câu giờ — rằng sự giải quyết tương tự đã đến nếu tôi ngồi yên trong một phòng khác — tôi nghĩ có điều gì đó cụ thể hơn xảy ra. Đi bộ cạnh nhau, hay thậm chí một mình, thay đổi hình học của xung đột. Một cuộc tranh luận đối mặt giữ bạn hướng về thể chất về phía người kia, điều này giữ sự chú ý vào họ chứ không phải vào vấn đề. Chuyển động phá vỡ sự định hướng cố định đó. Mắt bạn nhìn về phía trước thay vì nhìn người bạn không đồng ý, và nhịp điệu của bước chân làm điều gì đó với nhịp điệu nội tâm của suy nghĩ. Nghiên cứu từ nhiều quốc gia, bao gồm các nghiên cứu được thực hiện ở Nhật Bản và Thụy Điển, đã phát hiện ra rằng những người đi bộ cùng nhau đạt được thỏa thuận dễ dàng hơn những người ngồi đối diện nhau. Chuyển động về phía trước của cơ thể dường như khuyến khích một loại tư duy hướng về phía trước trong tâm trí.

Điều tôi đã nhận ra qua nhiều năm là cuộc đi bộ hiệu quả nhất khi bạn không cố gắng sử dụng nó. Nếu bạn ra ngoài với ý định có chủ đích để giải quyết tranh cãi, bạn mang theo cuộc tranh cãi bên mình và nó đi theo bạn xuống phố. Cuộc đi bộ thực sự giúp ích là khi bạn để nó đi trong một lúc — khi bạn cho phép bản thân chú ý đến ánh sáng trên các tòa nhà, nghe tiếng bồ câu, bước qua vũng nước. Khi bạn trở về, vấn đề không biến mất, nhưng bạn đã thay đổi đủ để có thể nhìn nó từ góc độ hơi khác. Dù xung đột có thể có đúng cùng kích thước, bạn đã trở nên lớn hơn một chút so với nó, đó là tất cả những gì bạn thực sự cần.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the third conditional with inverted clause order?
options: The same resolution would arrived if I had sat quietly in another room.
The same resolution would have arrived if I sat quietly in another room.
The same resolution would have arrived if I would have sat quietly in another room.
The same resolution would have arrived if I had sat quietly in another room.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional: result clause + 'would have + past participle'; condition clause + 'if + had + past participle'. Never use 'would' in the 'if' clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Third conditional: would have + PP in result; had + PP in if-clause; no 'would' in if-clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses present perfect with 'over' to describe cumulative experience?
options: What I have noticed over many years is that the walk works best when you don't try.
What I noticed over many years is that the walk works best when you don't try.
What I have notice over many years is that the walk works best when you don't try.
What I had noticed over many years is that the walk works best when you don't try.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect (have + past participle) with 'over many years' describes cumulative, ongoing observation up to the present.
mcQuestions.hint: Cumulative observation: have + noticed + over many years.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'even though' to introduce a concession?
options: Even though the conflict may be exactly the same size, but you have become a little larger than it.
Even though the conflict may be exactly the same size, you have become a little larger than it.
Even the conflict may be exactly the same size, you have become a little larger than it.
Even though the conflict may be exactly same size, you have become a little larger than it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even though + clause, main clause' — no extra 'but'; 'the same size' requires the definite article.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Even though' clause + comma + main clause — no 'but'; keep 'the' before 'same size'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'seem to + infinitive' correctly?
options: Forward motion seems encouraging a kind of forward thinking in the mind.
Forward motion seems to encouraging a kind of forward thinking in the mind.
Forward motion seems to encourage a kind of forward thinking in the mind.
Forward motion seeming to encourage a kind of forward thinking in the mind.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Seem to + bare infinitive' is the standard structure; not 'seem + gerund' or 'seeming'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Seem to + bare infinitive': not gerund, not 'to + -ing'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'might + seem' for a tentative claim?
options: Although it might seems that walking simply buys time, something more happens.
Although it might seemed that walking simply buys time, something more happens.
Although it might to seem that walking simply buys time, something more happens.
Although it might seem that walking simply buys time, something more happens.
mcQuestions.explanation: Modal 'might' + bare infinitive 'seem'; no -s, no -ed, no 'to' after 'might'.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal + bare infinitive: might seem — no -s, no -ed, no 'to'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'rather than' with parallel gerunds?
options: Your eyes are looking forward rather than at the person you disagree with.
Your eyes are looking forward rather than looking the person you disagree with.
Your eyes look forward rather than looking the person you disagree with.
Your eyes are looking forward rather than to look at the person.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Rather than' here contrasts a direction ('forward') with a prepositional phrase ('at the person') — parallel structure is maintained.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Rather than' contrasts: looking forward rather than at [someone] — parallel prepositions.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'allow + object + to-infinitive' correctly?
options: You allow yourself noticing the light on buildings.
You allow yourself to notice the light on buildings.
You allow yourself notice the light on buildings.
You allow to yourself to notice the light on buildings.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Allow + object + to-infinitive': allow yourself to notice — not gerund, not bare infinitive, no extra 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Allow' takes object + to-infinitive: allow yourself to notice.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'tend to + infinitive' in the present?
options: Details have faded in the way that argument details tend doing.
Details have faded in the way that argument details tend to doing.
Details have faded in the way that argument details tend to do.
Details have faded in the way that argument details tends to do.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tend to + bare infinitive'; the subject 'argument details' is plural, so 'tend' (not 'tends') is correct.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a present perfect for a discovery with present relevance?
options: Research has found that people walking together reached agreements more easily.
Research found that people walking together have reached agreements more easily.
Research have found that people walking together reach agreements more easily.
Research has found that people walking together reach agreements more easily.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Research has found' (present perfect, 'research' is uncountable and takes singular verb); the reported finding uses present simple for a general truth.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Research' (uncountable) takes 'has found' (not 'have found'); reported finding: present simple.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'carry + object + with' in a metaphorical sense?
options: You carry the argument with you and it follows you down the street.
You carry the argument with yourself and it follows you down the street.
You carry the argument with you and it is following you down the street.
You are carrying the argument with you and it has followed you down the street.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present simple is used throughout for a general pattern; 'with you' (not 'with yourself') is the natural expression here.
mcQuestions.hint: General pattern: present simple throughout; 'carry … with you' (not 'yourself').

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'could + not + have + told' to describe a past uncertainty?
options: I could not told you exactly when or why the anger left.
I could not have told you exactly when or why the anger left.
I couldn't have tell you exactly when or why the anger left.
I could not have told exactly when or why the anger left you.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Could not have + past participle' for a past impossibility or inability; 'told you' (indirect object 'you' before the question clause).
mcQuestions.hint: Past inability: could not have + past participle; indirect object 'you' before the embedded question.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why might walking side by side be more effective for resolving conflict than sitting face to face? What does this suggest about the role of physical environment in emotional and social interactions?
